data.title: OUSA: A Low-Beta, Quality Vehicle Unlikely To Outpace IVV
data.description:
I believe that the ALPS O'Shares U.S. Quality Dividend ETF (OUSA) does not possess a mix of characteristics that would justify a bullish stance, so I leave the Hold rating I assigned to it in June 2021 and maintained a few times throughout the years, including in the previous article in June 2025, unchanged.

In other words, I expect OUSA to underperform the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year (i.e., deliver only a mid-single-digit gain, while IVV should hopefully achieve a high single-digit gain in my base case).

It is my observation that the markets have recently rediscovered the buzzword of February–March 2020: complacency. The buy-the-dip mentality (having a moment as of March 31) is now clashing with realism and even pessimism. So, it seems this is a moment when ETFs that have a value tilt, high quality, and a touch of low volatility should attract more attention from investors who have something of a capital preservation orientation with a goal of minimizing possible drawdowns without exiting equities completely.

However, the issue I see here is that vehicles that offer this combination have a significant risk of underperformance during recoveries, mostly because of their exposure to the low volatility factor. And OUSA has that problem too—its historical upside capture of 78.25% (with IVV used as a benchmark) is a warning that even if it slightly outperforms IVV in a scenario with markets moving from euphoria they entered on March 31 to deep depression, it will more likely fail to capture its upside during the post-conflict recovery, leading to underperformance this year and beyond.

However, I should clarify that I am not constructing a bearish thesis today for two reasons.

  • First, the trajectory of the conflict is highly uncertain. And any clear signs of de-escalation will, without a doubt, pulverize bearish bets in the blink of an eye. For example, as I am writing this on March 31, the S&P 500 has rallied around 2.75% amid reports that "Iran reiterates willingness to end war but needs guarantees." Nevertheless, I am not buying the euphoria, as I do believe more evidence on the exact steps from all the parties involved is necessary.
  • Second, even though OUSA tends to underperform IVV over the long term (i.e., it lagged by more than 4% in annualized return over June 2020–February 2026), this is not a fundamentally weak strategy with consistently lackluster returns. It has a lot of positives (i.e., a relatively comfortable downside capture ratio), so there is no reason significant enough to construct a bearish opinion on it.

OUSA Strategy and Portfolio

As I have already discussed in the previous pieces, OUSA changed its strategy in June 2020, when it replaced the FTSE USA Qual/Vol/Yield Factor 5% Capped Index with the O’Shares U.S. Quality Dividend Index. In this regard, I ignore the performance delivered by the fund from its inception in July 2015 to June 2020.

What are the main principles of its current strategy? As explained in the summary prospectus available on its website:

The Underlying Index is constructed using a proprietary, rules-based methodology designed to select equity securities from the S-Network US Equity Large-Cap 500 Index that have exposure to the following four factors: 1) quality, 2) low volatility, 3) dividend yield and 4) dividend quality.

Interestingly, for the quality ingredient, the index provider not just assesses the basic profitability metrics but also monitors the robustness of financial position:

The “quality” factor is calculated by combining measures of profitability and leverage with the objective of identifying companies with strong profitability and balance sheets.

I believe it is a sensible decision to add these considerations to the analysis, as it more likely improves OUSA's risk metrics, making its maximum drawdowns a little less deep and downside capture slightly smaller (we will be discussing these parameters shortly).

Importantly, investors should not overlook that the index ignores three GICS sectors, namely energy, real estate, and materials. As I have already explained in the previous article, this fact

...is a good thing, but it also makes it almost impossible for the ETF to benefit from higher oil prices, which sometimes can solidly contribute to returns.

And as to the most recent returns, we clearly see that no oil & gas exposure has been in part responsible for OUSA's quite lackluster performance since the beginning of the Iran war. For context, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), a bellwether name in the dividend ETF universe, has been navigating a triple-digit oil price environment more confidently thanks to its significant footprint in the sector (17.2% allocation as of March 31), though also not without losses.

So, what is the product of OUSA's strategy, and how do its factor and sector exposures augur for its returns? As of March 27, there were 100 equities in its portfolio, with the top ten having around 42.3% weight. Importantly, the current portfolio has a 92.4% overlap with the version that I assessed in June 2025, so the corollary here is that exposures do not change much over time (17% turnover partly confirms that).

OUSA's key holding is Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), accounting for about 5.81% of the net assets. JNJ is a low-beta, high-quality stock with around a 4.6% earnings yield. And I would say that this is how the entire OUSA portfolio can be defined: compared to that of IVV, it is offering a tilt toward low volatility (i.e., a 0.65 24-month weighted average beta coefficient), mostly higher quality, and value characteristics, as the data in the table below summarizes perfectly.

And this is in part the consequence of its sector preferences: compared to IVV, OUSA is materially underweight in IT and overweight in financials (the key contributor to the earnings yield), among others. Four sectors, including utilities, are ignored.

However, what OUSA sadly does not have are GARP characteristics, which I dislike, as I expect this issue to restrain its ability to capture IVV's upside during a possible market recovery.

Metric OUSA IVV
PEG Ratio (calculated with the weighted average P/E in the numerator and the forward EPS growth rate in the denominator) 2.091 1.199
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.77 0.52
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.85 1.03

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Dividends: OUSA vs. IVV

What are the dividend characteristics of the OUSA portfolio? Compared to IVV, its portfolio is ahead on all Quant Dividend grades, namely Safety, Growth, Yield, and Consistency. Besides, the OUSA portfolio has a slightly higher weighted average dividend yield and a 5-year dividend CAGR.

Are these characteristics supportive of a bullish stance? I would not say that they are unique or outstanding, especially considering how small OUSA's dividend yield is (the ETF itself is yielding only 1.49%). In other words, there is no argument here that would allow me to construct a bullish view from.

OUSA Performance

Since the strategy change, OUSA has been demonstrating performance that is typical for a strategy at the intersection of quality, value, and low volatility. In other words, it offers some protection on the downside (i.e., during a bear market) but fails to keep pace with the S&P 500 ETF during recoveries, which results in quite unappealing (but not completely lackluster) long-term returns.

More specifically, since June 1, 2020, it has underperformed IVV by around 46.33% despite being less afflicted by the 2022 bear market.

What is more disappointing is that since March 19, 2021, OUSA has trailed the iShares Morningstar Value ETF (ILCV), which tracks the Morningstar US Large-Mid Cap Broad Value Index, which is among the indices that OUSA compares its performance to as per the prospectus. For clarity, I selected March 19 as a starting point since ILCV changed its strategy and began to track a new index on that date.

So, looking at the data below, we see that although OUSA's maximum drawdown in 2022 was -19.4% (and IVV's was 4.5% deeper) and it captured only 86.31% of IVV's downside during June 2020–February 2026, it still trailed IVV by 4.27% over the period as it captured just 78.25% of its upside. It was also incapable of delivering higher risk-adjusted returns (assessed using Sharpe and Sortino ratios).

In other words, a hypothesis that we can construct here using the historical data is that even though its drawdown in case the S&P 500 reverses lower in the coming days and weeks should be more comfortable, it is unlikely that it will beat IVV during recovery and beyond.

Final Thoughts

All in all, I still stand on my opinion expressed a few times in the past that OUSA has a sensible strategy but no alpha potential owing to its factor mix and historical performance, which we can use to cautiously hypothesize about its potential for future returns. There is a chance that thanks to the low beta, relatively high earnings yield, and high quality of its portfolio, it might do better than IVV in the escalation scenario, but I suppose no exposure to the energy sector will be more of a detractor. Finally, OUSA's expense ratio of 48 bps is rather high to my taste.

data.title: Seagate: Good Company, But I Don't Trust The Story At This Price
data.description:
Seagate (STX) is a case that gives me mixed feelings. The company certainly managed to reinvent itself and is surfing a very interesting momentum and knows how to create shareholder value. But at the same time, it is a technology that is not so “appealing,” I don't just mean within its storage niche, but also when we compare it to other tech stocks like NVIDIA (NVDA).

And still, even with this somewhat more mature profile, the valuation returned to high levels, precisely because at this point, the market has already priced Seagate's growth into the stock. And this valuation being anchored in high expectations for the coming years gives me a bit of concern. I mean, if something happens and Seagate fails to beat estimates or at least deliver something close, a drawdown could occur.

Seagate Changed (For the Better)

Seeing Seagate stock rise so much lately surprised me. It wasn't exactly a bad company, but it certainly wasn't a “hot” thesis like it is now.

A few years ago, we saw revenue declining, but with better margins, i.e., there was even shareholder value creation, but I saw—actually, I believe the market in general saw—Seagate as a company in a more mature stage and close to the “decline stage.” This is because Seagate's core is the Hard Drive, a technology that had been losing a lot of ground to the SSD (Solid State Drive).

Note how the CAGRs (Compound Annual Growth Rates) of the financials behaved over the last 10 years: revenue fell practically 2% every year, while EBITDA rose 5%, showing that although the company was no longer in its growth prime, it was still managing to be more efficient and extract value.

Taking a step back to explain the reason for this good momentum, hyperscalers (which Seagate calls CSPs (Cloud Service Providers)) like Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT) started buying HDDs (Hard Drives) for data centers again. The reason is quite straightforward: SSDs are faster, but not all storage in this type of operation needs to be fast; sometimes they just need to be cheaper, efficient enough in energy consumption, and have large storage options, and that is what Seagate offers.

It turns out that this YoY growth you see in the table above is just the “tip of the iceberg.” It's not as if Seagate recovered “out of nowhere” and delivered 25% growth in revenue. HDD shipments rose 26%, but we are talking about agreements with important customers for the 2027 calendar year and with some discussions about 2028.

In other words, this 25% revenue growth shouldn't happen in just an isolated quarter.

For instance, much is being said about Seagate's HAMR technology, basically a technology to manage to have more storage in the same space and with lower electricity consumption. And it is truly impressive; to illustrate, Seagate showed that with HAMR, “at the data center level,” it is 25k units versus previously 42k units in a space of 240 sq ft versus 400 sq ft and still spending 1M kWh/y less (for a deployment of 1 exabyte).

I'm not saying this just to be more technical but to show that Seagate not only has rising revenue but is advancing technologically even further, and the cloud companies that are building Data Centers know it and want to take advantage of it. Mozaic 3+ HAMR HDDs have already been tested and have completed qualifications by most US CSPs, according to Seagate. This is important because it basically means that companies like Microsoft or Amazon were testing the product for a long period (perhaps months) and gave the approval that the product is good, meaning they can now buy the hard drive in bulk—thousands or millions of units in the coming years. As for Mozaic 4, it is also already in testing, and the expectations are that the volume ramp will begin in Q3.

Again, returning to where it matters for the shareholder, all of this builds an expectation that in the next few years Seagate will have very solid demand from these CSPs. Consequently, it should continue maintaining high operating leverage, with operating margins close to the ~30% it has been managing to deliver in recent quarters, and generate massive free cash flow.

2 Uncertainties for Seagate

The market likes to say that Seagate is a duopoly, and it's true. In general terms, it is basically Seagate and Western Digital (WDC) that are massive producers of this type of HDD. In fact, both have very similar numbers. They are traded at a forward P/E of ~20x and recently presented revenue growth of ~25% - 30%.

But returning to competition, as much as these two companies really are the main ones in the market, this does not mean that the competition is only between them. In a way, it is, because now Data Centers also want HDDs. But SSD companies will also chase after them.

This is one of the uncertainties I see for the case. Obviously, I don't think an alternative will emerge “out of nowhere” in the coming months, but in the next 5 years, perhaps something will. Even advancing technologically, one of Seagate's strong points is that HDDs are several times cheaper than SSDs. This opens up a lot of room for competition because this type of market is quite dynamic, not only on the supply side but on the demand side as well.

Think, for example, that if in the next 5 years, some company develops a solution that delivers an SSD that is “only” 2x more expensive than an HDD, perhaps companies will go back to buying SSDs instead of HDDs. Similarly, let's assume that the “AI” trend that needs so much volume starts to fade. I'm not even talking about AI fading; I'm talking about companies like Microsoft becoming more efficient in data usage and now preferring speed over price and storage volume. This would also be bad for Seagate.

We don't even need to go that far, thinking about a technological disruption or a change in trend; if we are talking about a scenario where the supply of SSDs normalizes, that is already something bad for Seagate. This is because, at the moment, there is a giant shortage of SSDs; this not only pushes the price way up but also makes companies have to go after other alternatives (Seagate's HDDs). Another point is that even being a duopoly, perhaps in a second moment, Western Digital and Seagate will have to fight on price, and that could be a problem for the operating margin.

All this for me is still a very cloudy scenario. I am not trying to say that Seagate's revenue will start to decline, but we are in a very dynamic market, with many companies investing in R&D to develop new products and many companies building infrastructure to increase supply and surf this hype. So in 3-5 years the price and demand for this type of product can change a lot.

A second risk, which is linked to these trends I just mentioned, is the CapEx of big techs. As much as I believe that there will still be a growing demand for tokens in the coming years and decades, the level of big tech CapEx is very high. So after the scenario normalizes a bit, this CapEx will fall. It's hard to say when, but I don't think it's unlikely that in the next few years some companies will already lower the level of investment in expansion. Of course, after building so many Data Centers, there is still the cost of maintenance, upgrades to new HDDs and SSDs, replacement of those that broke (refresh cycle), etc. But my point is, if the expansion CapEx of these cloud providers falls 30%, that is already enough to affect Seagate. A lower appetite in general will already affect Seagate significantly.

This goes totally against the current valuation and market estimates for Seagate. Until the fiscal year of 2029, the market believes in revenue growth between 15.9% (in 2027) up to something closer to ~25% (with margin expansion). What happens if in 2029 cloud companies start signaling that CapEx will go down? What happens if trends change a bit in the HDD market? If Seagate grows “only” 10%, will the market “punish” the stock with a relevant drawdown?

For this last question, I believe so. Note that here expectations matter a lot. STX stock is being traded at 19x earnings for the fiscal year of 2027, and historically, top line growth was much lower than 10%. But still, for the current valuation and “only” 10% growth in this scenario, it would be bad, because the market expects more than double that level.

Bottom Line

I don't think Seagate is a bad company, as I mentioned. I'm also not trying to predict any crisis or disruption of Seagate. Especially because, as I mentioned in the first section, Seagate really managed to change its level by advancing technologically, signing good contracts, deepening its moat, and, in general, being a better company for the shareholder.

My point here is that the market estimates are already good, and the scenario for the coming years seems anything but clear for HDD demand. And another point worth mentioning is that there are several ways to ride this AI trend, such as Nvidia, which, in my view, is a way with more “quality,” with slightly more predictable growth and a valuation that is still reasonable, although with risks that are to some extent similar.

Because of this, I don't think Seagate stock is attractive at the moment, and for the beginning of coverage, I am giving it a hold rating (a slightly more neutral stance regarding the stock).

data.title: Japanese Business Sentiment Remains Solid, Supporting April BoJ Hike
data.description:
Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Business sentiment remains relatively firm, but outlook is cautious

Sentiment among large Japanese manufacturers rose to 17 in the first quarter (vs. revised 16 in 4Q25 survey, market consensus), while non-manufacturers’ sentiment index stayed flat at 36 (vs. revised 36 in 4Q25, 33 market consensus).

The outlook indices for manufacturing and non-manufacturing declined relative to the fourth quarter of 2025 but still beat market consensus. The outlook for general machinery and electrical machinery improved, while expectations worsened for iron and steel, motor vehicles, and basic materials.

We believe the robust semiconductor cycle is driving positive sentiment in the overall manufacturing sector. Yet sentiment declined in sectors more closely tied to commodities. We believe that stronger-than-expected Tankan survey results indicate that business confidence hasn’t yet turned significantly negative.

The Manufacturing PMI delivered a similar story. It was revised up to 51.6 from its flash 51.4, even though this is below February’s 53. It has remained in expansion for three consecutive months. However, output and new orders declined, making the outlook cloudy.

Today's Tankan showed that the recent rise in oil prices may raise inflationary pressure in the future. The output price index indicates higher prices are expected in both the short and medium terms.

The Bank of Japan will pay more attention to the price component. This is in line with the BoJ's view that underlying inflation is approaching 2%, supporting further policy normalisation. We expect the BoJ to deliver a 25 bp hike in April.

Output prices are expected to rise steadily in the medium term

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Stasis In The U.S. Labor Market: A Peculiar Situation
data.description:
Total separations drop to the lowest since 2015 beyond the lockdown months.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) of 21,000 business locations, released today by the Bureau of Labor Statistics, tracks labor market churn: how many people quit their jobs, how many were fired or laid off, how many retired, died, or separated for other reasons, how many job openings those people left behind, and how many people were hired to fill those job openings.

Voluntary quits are very low, layoffs and discharges are very low, retirements and other discharges are very low. So, total separations have plunged to the lowest since 2015, with the exception of the lockdown months.

The low number of separations means fewer newly vacated jobs are left behind, so job openings are lower, and the number of hires to fill that lower number of job openings is lower.

The crackdown on illegal immigration and the tightening up of work-visa-related immigration also plays into this, as they shrank the supply of labor, making hiring more difficult. All this makes for a peculiar labor market.

Total separations – voluntary and otherwise – plunged in February to 4.97 million, the lowest since the lockdown months, and before then the lowest since 2015 (blue in the chart).

The three-month average, which irons out the month-to-month squiggles, dropped to 5.11 million, also the lowest since the lockdown months, and before then, since 2016 (red).

Total separations consist of voluntary quits (60% of total separations), layoffs and discharges (33% of total separations), and retirements and other separations (6% of total separations). More on them in a moment.

But during that decade, employment has risen substantially, and this low rate of separations shows that a form of stasis has spread over the labor market, an unusual equilibrium in the US labor market, which is efficient for employers. But it makes it hard for young workers to enter the job market.

Voluntary quits plunged in February for the second month in a row, to 2.97 million, the lowest since the lockdown months, and before then the lowest since 2017-2018 (blue in the chart below).

These are people who quit their jobs voluntarily, such as to take a better job somewhere else, but do not include people who retired, died while employed, etc., who are tracked separately. The three-month average dipped to 3.11 million (red).

Quits are the biggest source of the labor market churn and account for 60% of total separations. Fewer quits means fewer open slots left behind that would turn into job openings and then into hires to fill those job openings.

Layoffs & discharges ticked up to 1.71 million in February. The three-month average ticked up to 1.68 million, well below the prepandemic years, except for one month (Oct 2016). Only during the time of the labor shortages, and coming out of it, was the level consistently lower.

Getting fired for a variety of reasons, or for no reason, is a classic feature of the American labor market. Layoffs and discharges accounted for 33% of all separations. These low layoffs & discharges have been confirmed by other data, including very low unemployment insurance claims.

Retirements and other separations (including deaths while employed, etc.) fell to 277,000 in February. The 12-month average, which irons out the huge month-to-month squiggles, edged up to 297,000. The last few months were the lowest in the data going back 25 years! They account for about 6% of total separations.

Job openings are a result of total separations that have dropped to a decade low. So job openings have sunk below where they’d been in 2018-2019. In February they fell, giving up part of the jump in January. The three-month average, which irons that stuff out, was roughly unchanged for the third month in a row at 6.89 million openings.

The number of hires dropped in February, after the jumps in December and January. The three-month average dipped to 5.16 million, right where it had been in December and above November. So roughly stable for the past four months.

Most of these hires replaced workers who’d separated from the company, leaving open slots behind. Only a tiny portion were hired to fill newly created jobs. But the JOLTS data doesn’t track that; the employment report to be released this Friday attempts to track that.

These are low levels of hires, the lowest since before 2015 beyond the lockdown months. But with so few total separations – the lowest since 2015 – there are fewer job openings left behind, and less need to hire people to fill those openings.

Turnover in the labor force had exploded in 2021 and 2022 during the labor shortages. A lot of churn like that in the labor market is expensive and inefficient for employers. But it resulted in a reshuffling of the labor force, with more people switching to jobs and industries that were better fits for their aspirations and who were better fits for their employers, which allowed for the churn to calm down in this peculiar way.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fidelity Freedom 2045 Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update and Portfolio Review

  • Global equities advanced in Q4 amid economic expansion and strong corporate fundamentals. Non-U.S. equities led the broad-based rally amid improved manufacturing conditions and policies to support domestic growth and consumption. The U.S. Federal Reserve cut interest rates twice in Q4, given signs of softer employment conditions. Inflation remained above target. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury bond yields finished Q4 effectively unchanged at around 4.2%. Credit spreads remained low relative to history.

Total Return of Strategic Asset Classes Period Ending December 31, 2025

You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

U.S. Equities - Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index, Non-U.S. Equities - MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (Net Mass), U.S. Short-Term Inflation- Protected Bond - Bloomberg U.S. TIPS 0-5 Years Index, U.S. Long-Term Inflation- Protected Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation Notes: 5+ Years Index, U.S. Investment- Grade Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, Long-Term U.S. Treasury Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Long Treasury Index, International Bond - Bloomberg Global Agg Treasury ex USD, ex EM, RIC Capped, Float Adjusted USD H Index, Short-Term Debt - Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bill Index, Commodities - Bloomberg Commodity 3 Month Forward Index (TR)
  • Each Fidelity target-date strategy produced a gain in Q4. Performance as of year-end is displayed below (net of fees)*.

Quarterly Performance for Representative Funds Period Ending December 31, 2025

See the Performance Summary table in this review for performance reporting on all vintages. Funds shown in this chart are representative samples of where a target-date investor might be at various stages of their life.

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation positioning and security selection added value to the fund's performance versus the Composite index in Q4. The fund's overweight in non-U.S. equities meaningfully contributed, as investments in both developed-markets equities and emerging-markets equities outperformed U.S. equities during the quarter.
  • The impact of active security selection varied by asset class. The strong performance of U.S. large-cap managers resulted in a relative contribution from U.S. equities, whereas non-U.S. managers, in aggregate, underperformed the Composite index.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

  • Large-cap U.S. equity funds stood out to the upside this quarter, including the following notable relative contributors:
    • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue-chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the information technology sector contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter, especially out-of-benchmark positions in Sandisk and Ciena (CIEN), from the technology hardware & equipment industry. A non-benchmark position in private company Space Exploration Technologies, a maker of spacecraft and provider of satellite communications and launch capabilities, also notably contributed.
    • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund, managed by Matt Fruhan, emphasizes companies for which he believes long-term normalized earnings and free cash flow are underappreciated by investors. Security selection contributed to the fund's relative result, notably within the industrials and financials sectors. Within financials, overweight positions in Wells Fargo (WFC) and Bank of America (BAC) added relative value, while larger-than-benchmark stakes in GE Vernova (GEV) and United Parcel Service (UPS) stood out in the industrials sector.

Relative Detractor (representative across vintages):

  • The largest fund-level detractor:
    • Fidelity® Series Overseas Fund underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index. The fund is managed by Vincent Montemaggiore, who is valuation sensitive and focuses on higher-quality companies with durable growth. Security selection detracted this quarter, particularly within the industrials sector. Within the sector, an overweight in Thales (THLEY), a French company that provides advanced technologies, services and products to the defense, aerospace, space and cyber markets, hurt the fund's relative result most. An out-of-benchmark position in Baycurrent, one of Japan's largest consulting firms, also notably detracted, as did an overweight stake in Japanese food and biotechnology company Ajinomoto (AJINY).

Active Asset Allocation:

  • The fund outperformed the Composite index for the quarter, driven by active asset allocation positioning.

Outlook and Positioning

  • Target-date funds are long-term strategies designed to help keep participants on track toward their retirement goals through different market environments. Strategic asset allocation decisions are focused on providing age-appropriate asset allocation throughout participants' changing time horizons.
  • Our research highlights several macro-oriented themes that we believe underscore the need for portfolio diversification. Government debt, global fragmentation, aging demographics and new technology are likely to impact long-term growth and inflation. We believe diversification in fixed income and inflation-resistant assets remains attractive to hedge these risks.
  • In 2025, we announced an update to the glide path and strategic asset allocation of the strategies, based on our long-term views of participant behavior and our capital markets research. We increased exposure to equities for early-career investors and to equities and inflation-sensitive assets for investors near and in retirement. Implementation of the updated allocations is underway, and we expect to complete the transition within the next six to 12 months.
  • Active asset allocation positioning relative to the strategic benchmark reflects our intermediate-term views on assets that are mispriced relative to our estimate of fair value. Active risk in the funds is at the low end of the historical range, based on a view that elevated asset valuations, particularly for U.S. stocks, provide little cushion against near-term economic and geopolitical risk. We believe valuations outside of the AI epicenter are reasonable, and many sectors have improving growth expectations.
  • As of the end of 2025, the funds were overweight equities, albeit by a smaller magnitude since the start of the quarter. We maintain a preference for non-U.S. assets relative to U.S. assets, based on attractive valuations and the potential for non-U.S. assets to benefit from continued U.S. dollar weakness.
  • We added to fixed-income investments in the fourth quarter, and we view intermediate-term U.S. Treasuries as the most attractive part of the Treasury yield curve. The Funds remain underweight investment-grade bonds, as spreads are low relative to history.
  • Inflation remains sticky and upside risk may be underappreciated by investors. Supply-side indicators, including housing and labor markets, pressure the intermediate-term horizon, in our view. As a result, in Q4 we added to the funds' overweight in commodities.

References

  1. Life of Fund (LOF) if performance is less than 10 years. Fund inception date: 06/01/2006.
  2. This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund. data.title: Trump 2.0 Highfliers Fall Back To Earth
data.description:
The stock market saw its ups and downs in the first year of Trump 2.0, but some areas of the market went parabolic. Stocks related to quantum computing, robotics, crypto, space, rare earths, small modular reactors, and more saw triple and even quadruple-digit percentage gains in the span of months at various points between November 2024 and October 2025.

In the last five months, the fun has ended for the former high fliers, with most of them down at least 50% from their highs. The peak for the high fliers came on 10/15/25, about two weeks before the Nasdaq 100 made its last all-time high. Below, we break down the carnage.

In the Russell 3000, which includes more than 98% of publicly traded market cap in the US, there were 190 stocks that at least doubled from Election Day 2024 through October 15th, 2025. These 190 stocks are currently down an average of 44.5% from 52-week highs.

85 stocks gained more than 200% over the same period, and they're down an average of 53.7%. Narrowing down the list even more, there were 20 stocks up 500%+ that are down 64% from 52-week highs, and the 9 stocks that were up 1,000%+ have been cut an average of two-thirds.

Below is a list of the stocks that gained the most from Election Day 2024 through 10/15/25, along with how much they're currently down from 52-week highs.

Remember the quantum computing stocks like Rigetti (RGTI) and D-Wave (QBTS) that were up more than 4,000% at their highs? They've both fallen more than 70%. Or how about Microvast (MVST), the EV battery play that surged over 3,100% only to crater 80% from its peak?

Even Chaince Digital (CD), the crypto/blockchain name that exploded 1,125%, has since lost nearly 89% from its high, making it one of the biggest peak-to-trough collapses on the entire list.

The theme here is painfully consistent. Nearly every name that went parabolic post-election has given back a huge chunk. Nuclear power play Oklo (OKLO) gained 733% but is off 76%. Bloom Energy (BE) was up 922% and has since dropped 34%, one of the more resilient names on the list. Even Rocket Lab (RKLB) and Planet Labs (PL) are down 42% and 25%, respectively, from their highs.

The average gain for these stocks from Election Day through October 15th was a staggering 1,089%, yet the average decline from 52-week highs sits at 61%. Once again, this is a stark reminder that the higher they fly, the harder they tend to fall!

Below are one-year price charts of six select high-fliers to help you visualize the parabolic moves and subsequent plunge. The broader lesson here is one the market teaches every generation of new traders: parabolic moves may be thrilling to watch but can be treacherous to chase.

data.title: Kratos Defense: Leading The Charge In Hypersonics And Defense Tech (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it slightly overvalued. Since then, the stock has dipped around 35%, while the inflection point regarding hypersonic missiles is closer and the company is beating earnings estimates. This makes me to upgrade the company to buy.

Background

Kratos is an American technology company focused on the defense and national security sectors. Today the company has more than 30 programs organized into six different divisions that cover most of the main defense needs: unmanned systems, hypersonic, space, microwave and digital solutions, propulsion and power, and finally, weapon systems.

As mentioned in the previous article and confirmed by management during the last earnings call, the two main pillars of Kratos will be the XQ-58 Valkyrie, which is expected to ramp up production from 8 annually to 40 by the end of 2028, and the hypersonic franchise, which will be the most important growth driver going forward.

Kratos has already won the MACH‑TB 2.0 award, a five year agreement worth up to $1.45 billion. Moreover, management expects to win the development of Mach 5+ hypersonic missiles. This will position the company as the clear referent in the sector.

We are also hoping to receive an additional approximate $1 Billion hypersonic program related opportunity by the end of this year, which we believe will be sole source to Kratos as prime. Kratos’ hypersonic franchise is expected to be a primary driver of our expected future revenue growth.

In fact, the company has achieved a record backlog of $1.6 billion and a record opportunity pipeline of $13.7 billion. The increase in backlog comes despite revenues growing during the quarter which means new awards are outpacing billings and the new capacity additions, providing visibility for the future years. In fact, in Q4, the company’s book to bill ratio was 1.3, which shows Kratos is winning more work than it is delivering, and I expect it to grow even further if they secure additional hypersonic contracts. Over time this backlog should flow into revenue.

To meet all this expected growth, Kratos is investing heavily in new facilities and capacity with 2026 CapEx expected around $140 million. To finance such growth the company has recently issued shares raising more than $1 billion.

Financial Position and Valuation

Kratos has a strong financial position with $560 million in cash and no debt at the end of Q4 2025. Moreover, as mentioned before, in February 2026 it raised more than $1 billion, increasing its cash balance above $1.5 billion. More than enough to finance the next few years’ CapEx and to pursue some acquisitions as stated in the press release.

With all this cash and investing it in increasing production capacity and developing new products, I believe Kratos could become one of the relevant defense players in hypersonics, drones and space infrastructure in a few years, even if today it is still much smaller than the big primes.

Management has guided for 2026 revenues around $1.6 billion, implying almost a 20% increase over 2025. However, what is important is that most of that growth will come from the hypersonic segment doubling from 2025 with continued growth in 2027.

We are expecting to approximately double Kratos' hypersonic franchise revenues in 2026 over 2025 up to approximately $400 million and then potentially increase over 75% again in '27 up to approximately $700 million.

I expect the hypersonic segment to continue to drive Kratos’ growth and help the company to reach $5 billion in revenue by 2030.

Regarding valuation, Kratos is the most expensive one compared to peers, but I believe this is justified given the company’s profile and future expected growth. In fact, I consider current prices as attractive.

As mentioned in the previous article I believe that Kratos deserves at least a 4xP/S. Assuming revenue of $5 billion in 2030, this implies a $20 billion valuation. Applying some discount for being future revenues, I believe a $16 billion valuation is fair right now, implying a $85 fair value. There is a reduction compared to the previous article due to the shares issued in February.

I’ve used a 7.5% discount rate from 2030 to 2027 to discount the $20 billion 2030 valuation as I believe that in the current environment defense companies deserve a low discount rate; however, assuming a 10% discount rate provides a valuation of $14.5 billion, still providing around 20% appreciation potential.

Risks

Kratos’ main risk is still related to execution and scaling. The company is trying to grow very fast and investing in new facilities, production lines and developing new programs. Moreover, a significant part of its contracts are fixed price, so if costs rise or if there are problems in production margins could be negatively affected and deliveries delayed.

Finally, the elevated valuations make the stock highly volatile, so if growth disappoints or if there is a broader market correction, the shares could drop meaningfully.

Conclusion

Kratos is set to become one of the leading defense companies in its niches, with a wide range of products spanning different segments from hypersonic systems to drones. The hypersonic segment is inflecting higher and will be the main driver of growth going forward.

While valuation is still elevated, I consider it deserved and my fair value is $85 per share, representing a 20% upside For these reasons, I’m upgrading the company from neutral to a buy.

data.title: Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Elevated political uncertainty was front and center during the quarter, with the end of the longest-ever federal government shutdown. The shutdown disrupted economic data flow and added uncertainty to interest-rate expectations and risk markets. In addition, concerns about U.S. Federal Reserve independence escalated, due to political criticism of Chair Powell and speculation about his successor, and contributed to long-end interest-rate instability. Elevated tariffs and a weakening employment picture continued to pressure inflation and complicate the Fed's policy path. The Fed continued its rate-cutting cycle with two reductions of 25 basis points (bps) in the federal funds rate during the quarter. (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) The 175 bps in cuts since September 2024 left the rate in the 3.50%–3.75% range at year-end.

Returns were meaningfully positive across all asset classes during the quarter, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.66%, the Russell 2000 Index rising 2.19%, the MSCI ACWI (ex US) Index up 5.05%, the Bloomberg US Treasury Index returning 0.90% and the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index up 1.56%. Treasury yields were mixed during the quarter: lower by 14 bps in the 2-year space, flat in 5- and 10-year bonds and higher by 11 bps in 30-year issues, with the 10-year yield ending the quarter at 4.18%. The municipal yield curve flattened during the quarter, as yields increased on the short end, declined in the intermediate space and were relatively flat beyond 20 years. However, municipals ended the year with a meaningfully steeper curve. With mostly lower yields during the quarter, municipals outperformed Treasuries. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.56%, while the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index was higher by 0.90%.

Credit Quality (%) as of December 31, 2025 - Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund

AAA 9.4
AA 30.5
A 29.4

When two are available, the lower rating is used. If only one is available, that rating is used. If a security is Not Rated but has a rating by Kroll and/or DBRS, the same methodology is applied to those bonds that would otherwise be Not Rated. Bonds with no third-party rating are designated as Not Rated. Investments are primarily based on internal proprietary research and ratings assigned by our fixed income investment analysts. Therefore, securities designated as Not Rated do not necessarily indicate low credit quality, and for such securities the investment adviser evaluates the credit quality. Holdings of the portfolio other than bonds are categorized under Other. Credit ratings are subjective opinions of the credit rating agency and not statements of fact and may become stale or subject to change.

Due to rounding, percentages may not add up to 100.

The municipal market performed well across quality, maturity, sector and state. Municipals returned between 1.50% and 1.77% across the investment-grade quality spectrum, while the below-investment-grade index returned 1.15%. On the back of flat-to-lower yields, intermediate-to-long-maturity municipals outperformed their shorter counterparts. The municipal yield curve remained relatively steep, with a steepening curve a theme across much of 2025. The yield pickup (slope) between 5- and 30-year maturities ended the quarter at 183 bps vs. 103 bps at the beginning of the year.

On the technical front, new-issue supply reached over $580 billion in 2025, a new annual record. Issuance in 2026 is projected to surpass $500 billion for the third consecutive year. New issuance totaled $125 billion during the fourth quarter, 12% higher year over year. While supply was heavy, most new-issue deals continued to be met with strong demand and were typically repriced and over-subscribed. In addition, mutual funds, exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and separately-managed accounts (SMAs) continued to see investor inflows.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Columbia Total Return Municipal Income Fund underperformed its benchmark for the quarter. As intermediate-maturity bonds outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts, the fund's exposure to intermediate and longer-maturities – and underweight to shorter maturities - was additive to returns, as was positive yield carry. However, performance detractors included security selection in AA and BBB rated bonds. Selection in the special tax, airport and toll road also were a performance headwind. A modest positive contributor was an overweight position in nursing bonds.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future bond holdings are subject to risk.

Portfolio activity

Throughout the quarter, the fund capitalized on opportunities in both the new-issue and secondary markets. Purchases were largely focused on improving the quality, liquidity and distribution yield of the fund. The strategy took advantage of attractive opportunities when volatility presented, while also decreasing duration during strong rally environments. (Duration is a measure of a bond's sensitivity to changes in interest rates) While municipal spreads were not overly attractive from a valuation standpoint, yields remained desirable on both a historical and tax-equivalent basis. Due to ample supply and higher income opportunities being offered, the fund was able to capitalize on attractive credits in several sectors. The relatively steep slope of the municipal yield curve, especially the longer end, continued to provide a high level of income and an attractive total return opportunity.

Outlook

The quarter provided a favorable backdrop for municipal investors, with strong demand, supportive credit fundamentals, and curve-driven performance centered in intermediate maturities. While political and macroeconomic uncertainty persisted, municipals remained resilient relative to other fixed-income sectors, supported by robust fundamentals and compelling tax-equivalent yields.

We expect another active year in 2026, with elevated but absorbable supply, healthy demand and resilient credit. Performance, however, will hinge on curve and sector selection:

  • Rates/Curve: With a cautious rate-cutting forecast, short-end rates may drift lower while the long end remains range-bound, given deficits and policy uncertainty. While municipal credits are tight, the attractive absolute and relative yield environment continues to offer a positive investment opportunity.
  • Supply: Infrastructure and refundings argue for strong 2026 issuance, with a third consecutive year of over $500 billion in issuance expected.
  • Credit: Broadly constructive environment, but sector dispersion persists, as health care, higher education and tobacco are seeing pressure, while other sectors, such as water/sewer, high-quality general obligation bonds (GOs) and select transportation, are in a more favorable environment.
  • Politics: We continue to monitor the administration's changing priorities and policies and their potential impact on the municipal market. With recently heightened political activity, one should be prepared for continued market volatility.

There is room for additional price appreciation in the municipal market, but price returns will likely be tied to movements in Treasuries. We remain selective and focused on opportunities where we believe we are being appropriately compensated for the credit risk taken.

Despite elevated political and economic uncertainty, returns were meaningfully positive across most asset classes during the quarter.

Prepayment and extension risk exists because the timing of payments on a loan, bond or other investment may accelerate when interest rates fall or decelerate when interest rates rise which may reduce investment opportunities and potential returns. Non-investment-grade (high-yield or junk) securities present greater price volatility and more risk to principal and income than higher rated securities. data.title: NMZ: Aligned To Benefit From Lower Interest Rates
data.description:
Overview

With continued uncertainty related to global tensions, interest rates, and rising unemployment, we've seen elevated volatility throughout the equity markets. However, investors are capable of hedging against this uncertainty with high-quality income funds that are disconnected from the equity markets. This is where the Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ) comes in. The fund offers investors diverse exposure to income-producing securities while also providing some tax benefits for investors.

When I previously covered NMZ, I issued a buy rating due to the potential for capital appreciation. Shortly after that article, the fund did rise in value, but the recent pullback in the debt markets has caused NMZ to fall back into an attractive range. For instance, the fund now trades at a discount to NAV of 1.57%. For reference, the fund has historically traded at an average discount to NAV of 1.64% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund still trades at a fairly reasonable entry point for investors looking for a long-term income fund.

Since my last coverage, NMZ has also released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund and its overall value proposition going forward for retirees seeking stability. Following the recent pullback in share price, NMZ now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.8% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. However, the fund has a mixed history of dividend stability, so there's always the risk of lower payouts under the pressure of high interest rates. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that NMZ implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, NMZ has total managed assets of $1.62B that are spread across 907 different positions. The fund does buy municipal bonds, but in order to support the high dividend yield, NMZ also seeks out higher-yielding securities with poor credit ratings and strategically uses leverage to produce amplified income levels. NMZ has a listed management fee of 0.67% of assets, which is higher than a standard ETF but may be worth it considering the actively managed portfolio of assets.

The caveat is that NMZ focuses on finding securities that haven't been formally rated. This provides a window of opportunity for management to find bonds at a discount even if they have solid fundamentals. For instance, the portfolio reveals that 50.3% of the portfolio is allocated to securities without a credit rating. However, investors should still remain cautious that some of these securities may still be riskier in nature, and unfortunately, we do not have any transparency. So I want to emphasize that a position in NMZ is putting trust in management's internal underwriting and sourcing process for its securities.

To minimize risks, NMZ tries to focus on areas that are related to infrastructure. A look at the portfolio reveals that this includes things like education and civic organizations, housing, and transportation securities. Since NMZ combines its focus on unrated securities with healthy areas of the market, this approach is solid for retirees seeking value and stability.

Most important, 33.5% of the portfolio is allocated to an asset labeled as tax obligation/limited securities. This type of security means that the bonds are supported by a municipality's ability to levy taxes within their respective limits. These are bonds that are backed by revenue received from property and sales taxes.

Lastly, NMZ is a reasonable choice for retirees due to the allocation to positions with long-dated maturities. By locking in its portfolio with long-dated positions, the fund can secure long-term positions that can generate enough income to support the high yield. The tradeoff is that these longer-dated positions can be more sensitive to fluctuations in the interest rate environment, which I will circle back to. The fund's breakdown by maturity is as follows:

  • 30+ Years: 17%
  • 20-29 Years: 57%
  • 10-19 Years: 17%
  • 5-9 Years: 7%
  • 0-4 Years: 2%

Maintain Realistic Expectations

Furthermore, I want to emphasize that a fund like NMZ makes the most sense for investors that specifically want to focus on income generation above all. Since the fund doesn't actually own any common equities, NMZ is not structurally capable of participating in the positive momentum of market indices. So if you are a young investor seeking a high yield with some potential for meaningful upside, NMZ may not be the most efficient choice for you. While upside growth is technically possible, it will not be correlated with equities.

To demonstrate this, we can refer to the fund's performance over the last decade. Although market indices continuously raced to all-time highs over the last ten years, NMZ's share price has declined by nearly 26% over the same time frame. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return is only 29.5%, which is quite disappointing. However, I would argue that entry into NMZ is very important, and this is one of the best times over the last decade to accumulate shares.

The fact that NMZ is allocated to long-dated maturities is one of the primary reasons why the fund's share price has remained suppressed since 2022. More importantly, these downside risks are exacerbated by the fact that NMZ utilizes leverage as a way to amplify its earnings when possible. However, leverage also has the potential to amplify downside risks, which is exactly what happened once the value of NMZ's positions declined as interest rates were hiked. NMZ currently has effective leverage that now amounts to 40.22% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$814.2M.

This is an incredibly aggressive use of leverage during this time of elevated interest rates, which is correlated to the fund's inability to provide any capital appreciation in recent years. The key is to accumulate funds like NMZ when it trades at the least appealing part of the market cycle. Higher interest rates cause a smaller operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from its positions. As a result, this causes net investment income levels to fall and large net realized losses to occur.

These issues are confirmed when looking at the most recently available annual report for the 2025 period. Net investment income amounted to $0.60 per share, while net realized losses landed at $0.39 per share. Back when interest rates were at near-zero levels in 2021, NMZ was capable of generating higher net investment income of $0.72 per share because of healthier operating spreads. Looking back on prior years, we can also see that NMZ experienced massive net realized losses of $4.53 per share in 2022, likely due to the Fed aggressively hiking interest rates.

The most ideal conditions for NMZ would be a scenario where interest rates are low because this means lower interest rates and an increased probability of its positions rising in value. The vulnerability to high interest rates has led to the fund struggling to recover from the steep decline in NAV a few years ago. For reference, here are the annual NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $14.71 per share
  • 2022: $9.85 per share
  • 2023: $8.37 per share
  • 2024: $11.15 per share
  • 2025: $10.65 per share

With these observations in mind, NMZ is aligned to see much better performance as interest rates trend lower over the next twelve months. The only challenge is that we don't know what direction interest rates will go. For instance, the Fed decided to keep rates unchanged during their latest meeting. With the continued pressure of the debt markets, rising unemployment, geopolitical tensions, and rising defaults, the anticipation is that interest rates will eventually trend lower to provide some relief. When this happens, the fund may finally maintain positive growth momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels. As we can see in the chart below, NMZ shares a direct inverse relationship with the federal funds rate.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0655 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.8%. While this yield is enticing for retirees, there are certainly some tradeoffs that must be considered. Looking back over the last ten years of the payout history, we can see that NMZ's distribution amount has continually shifted alongside the interest rate environment. Unfortunately, this is the nature of income-focused funds that tie income to the interest rate environment.

Furthermore, the same annual report for 2025 also indicates that payouts may be a bit too generous at this time. For instance, NMZ reported earnings of $0.21 per share for 2025. However, the fund paid out $0.79 per share over the same time frame, ultimately contributing towards the underlying NAV erosion. The unfortunate reality is that the fund can continue to struggle to generate sufficient income as long as interest rates are high. Therefore, there is still a risk that payouts will be reduced at some point in 2026.

Despite the inconsistent distribution coverage, there is a partial benefit here: return of capital. When the fund fails to support distributions with its actual earnings, it will lean on return of capital distributions that come directly from its NAV. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. Instead, these distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allow any tax impacts to be deferred until the time of sale. Furthermore, NMZ gets the majority of its income from bonds that are federally tax-exempt, although investors may still have to deal with state taxes.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I still believe that NMZ offers an attractive opportunity for accumulation. The fund is being heavily suppressed by the higher interest rate environment and is likely to see positive growth momentum once interest rates start to trend lower over the next twelve months. The longer-dated maturities are likely to see capital appreciation in a lower-rate environment since the fund's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and yield collected from securities will improve. The primary risk comes from the fund paying out more than it earns at this time, which can lead to additional NAV erosion if interest rates do not trend lower in the near term.
